summary: An Azure Function (timer-triggered, nightly) that queries GPT-4o via TRAPI using Managed Identity to retrieve the 1985 New York Yankees roster and stores the result in an Azure Storage Blob. Authentication must be keyless (no API keys); all access uses Azure Managed Identity and RBAC.
assumptions: Python will be used as the Azure Function runtime language (v2 programming model).
TRAPI refers to the Azure AI model inference REST API (Azure OpenAI or AI Foundry endpoint) accessible via Managed Identity (no API keys).
The nightly schedule is defined as midnight UTC via a CRON expression (0 0 * * *).
A Consumption or Flex Consumption hosting plan is acceptable given the low-frequency, short-duration workload.
The Azure Storage Blob container and account will be created as part of this project (not pre-existing).
GitHub Actions will be used for CI/CD based on the existing .github directory structure.
Bicep will be used for Infrastructure as Code.
Application Insights will be provisioned alongside the Function App for observability.
Plan approval is manual; the spec does not specify auto-approval.
open_questions: What is the exact TRAPI endpoint URL and Azure AI resource name to call GPT-4o?
What blob naming convention should be used (e.g., timestamped JSON file per run or overwrite a fixed file)?
What Azure subscription, resource group naming convention, and region are required?
Should the function overwrite the blob on each run or append/version results?
Are there any data retention or compliance requirements for the stored roster blob?
What constitutes failure for alerting — TRAPI errors, empty response, blob write failure?

waves.name: Foundation
waves.objective: Provision all Azure infrastructure and approve the delivery plan so that development can begin on a stable target environment.
entry_criteria: Azure subscription available with sufficient quota
Target region and naming conventions agreed
exit_criteria: Azure Resource Group, Storage Account, and Function App provisioned
System-assigned Managed Identity enabled on Function App
RBAC assignments for Storage and TRAPI in place and verified

waves.name: Core Build
waves.objective: Implement all application code: TRAPI GPT-4o client, blob storage writer, nightly timer-triggered Azure Function, and CI/CD pipeline.
entry_criteria: Azure resources reachable from local dev and CI environment
exit_criteria: Function retrieves 1985 Yankees roster from GPT-4o via TRAPI
Roster written to Azure Blob Storage as structured output
Timer trigger fires on nightly schedule (validated via manual invoke)
CI/CD pipeline builds, lints, and deploys the Function App on merge to main

waves.name: Hardening
waves.objective: Enforce security controls, add structured observability, and implement reliability patterns (retries, error handling, idempotency).
exit_criteria: No API keys or secrets in code or environment variables — all auth via Managed Identity
Structured logs emitted to Application Insights with correlation IDs
Retry logic with exponential back-off implemented for TRAPI and blob write calls
Alert configured for function execution failures

waves.name: Release Readiness
waves.objective: Validate correctness through automated tests and complete a production rollout with monitoring verified active.
entry_criteria: All acceptance criteria from spec documented as test cases
exit_criteria: Unit and integration tests pass in CI
End-to-end nightly run validated in production (blob written, roster complete)

tasks.title: Approve implementation task plan
tasks.description: Review and sign off on the full task plan, wave sequencing, assumptions, and open questions with the project owner (rciapala) before implementation begins. Resolve all open questions that are blockers for INF-01.
outputs: Approved plan with resolved blocker questions documented
acceptance_criteria: Plan reviewed and approved by rciapala
TRAPI endpoint and Azure resource naming confirmed
Blob naming/overwrite strategy decided
Target Azure subscription and region confirmed
risks: Unresolved open questions (TRAPI endpoint, blob naming) block INF-01 and APP-01

tasks.title: Provision Azure infrastructure with Bicep IaC
tasks.description: Author and deploy Bicep templates to provision the Azure Resource Group, Storage Account (with blob container), Function App (Consumption plan), and Application Insights workspace. All resources tagged with project and owner metadata.
outputs: Bicep templates in /infra directory
Deployed: Resource Group, Storage Account, Blob Container, App Service Plan, Function App, Application Insights
acceptance_criteria: Bicep templates pass `az bicep build` with no errors
All resources provisioned in target subscription and region
Storage Account has blob container created
Function App is deployed in Consumption plan
Application Insights connected to Function App
Resources tagged with project=1985-NY-Yankees and owner=rciapala
risks: Quota limits in target region for Consumption plan

tasks.title: Configure Managed Identity and RBAC assignments
tasks.description: Enable system-assigned Managed Identity on the Function App and assign the required RBAC roles: Storage Blob Data Contributor on the Storage Account, and Cognitive Services OpenAI User (or equivalent) on the TRAPI/Azure AI resource. No API keys or connection strings to be used.
outputs: System-assigned Managed Identity enabled
RBAC role assignments applied and verified via `az role assignment list`
acceptance_criteria: Function App has system-assigned Managed Identity with a non-null principal ID
Storage Blob Data Contributor role assigned to MI on the Storage Account
Cognitive Services OpenAI User (or equivalent) role assigned to MI on the TRAPI resource
`az role assignment list` confirms all assignments
No API keys, connection strings, or secrets stored in app settings or code
risks: TRAPI resource may be in a different subscription or tenant, requiring cross-tenant RBAC
Role propagation delay can take up to 10 minutes after assignment

tasks.title: Implement TRAPI GPT-4o client module
tasks.description: Write a Python module that authenticates to the TRAPI (Azure AI model inference) endpoint using DefaultAzureCredential (Managed Identity) and submits a prompt to GPT-4o requesting the complete 1985 New York Yankees roster. Parse and return structured player data.
inputs: INF-02 RBAC assignments in place for local testing via az login
outputs: trapi_client.py module
Returns structured list of player dicts (name, position, number)
Unit tests for the module
acceptance_criteria: Module authenticates via DefaultAzureCredential with no API key
GPT-4o returns a non-empty list of 1985 Yankees players
Response is parsed into a consistent structured format (e.g., list of dicts)
Module raises a specific exception type on TRAPI errors for upstream handling
Unit tests mock DefaultAzureCredential and TRAPI response, all passing
risks: GPT-4o may return incomplete or hallucinated roster data — consider a validation step or prompt engineering
TRAPI rate limits may throttle repeated test calls

tasks.title: Implement Azure Blob Storage writer module
tasks.description: Write a Python module that uses DefaultAzureCredential (Managed Identity) to authenticate to Azure Blob Storage and writes the player roster as a JSON blob. Support configurable container name, blob name, and overwrite behavior via environment variables.
outputs: blob_writer.py module
JSON blob written to Azure Storage
Unit tests for the module
acceptance_criteria: Module authenticates via DefaultAzureCredential — no connection string or SAS token
Roster data written as valid JSON to the configured blob container
Blob name is configurable via environment variable
Overwrite behavior is deterministic and documented
Unit tests mock DefaultAzureCredential and BlobClient, all passing
risks: Incorrect RBAC role may cause 403 errors at runtime — test early with a live environment

tasks.title: Implement nightly timer-triggered Azure Function
tasks.description: Implement the Azure Function using the Python v2 programming model with a timer trigger set to fire nightly at midnight UTC (CRON: `0 0 * * *`). The function orchestrates the TRAPI client (APP-01) and blob writer (APP-02), logs execution status, and raises exceptions on failure for retry/alerting.
outputs: function_app.py with timer trigger
host.json and local.settings.json
Requirements.txt updated with all dependencies
Function deployable via CI/CD
acceptance_criteria: Timer trigger CRON expression set to `0 0 * * *` (midnight UTC)
Function invokes TRAPI client and blob writer in sequence
Successful run logs player count and blob URL at INFO level
Function raises exception (not silently swallows) on any step failure
Manual invoke via `func start` and Azure portal succeeds end-to-end
Function deploys successfully via CI/CD pipeline
risks: Cold-start latency on Consumption plan may cause timer to miss its window under high platform load
CRON expression format differs between v1 and v2 — verify with Azure docs

tasks.title: Set up GitHub Actions CI/CD pipeline
tasks.description: Create a GitHub Actions workflow that runs linting (flake8/ruff), unit tests (pytest), and deploys the Function App to Azure on merge to main using OIDC-based federated identity (no stored secrets). Include a slot-based or direct deploy strategy.
inputs: Azure Function App name and resource group (from INF-01)
GitHub OIDC federated credential configured on the MI or a deployment service principal
outputs: GitHub Actions workflow YAML (.github/workflows/deploy.yml)
CI pipeline green on first merge to main
acceptance_criteria: Workflow triggers on push to main
Lint and unit test steps run before deploy
Deploy uses azure/functions-action with OIDC login (no stored PAT or publish profile)
Failed lint or tests block deployment
Workflow status badge visible in README or results/
risks: OIDC federated credential setup requires Azure AD app registration or MI configuration — may need AAD permissions
First-time GitHub Actions OIDC setup can be time-consuming

tasks.title: Enforce keyless auth and secrets hygiene audit
tasks.description: Audit all code, configuration files, and GitHub Actions workflows to confirm zero API keys, connection strings, or secrets are present. Enforce this via a pre-commit hook or CI secret-scanning step. Document the Managed Identity auth flow.
inputs: All source files from APP-01, APP-02, APP-03
outputs: CI secret-scanning step (e.g., truffleHog or gitleaks) added to workflow
local.settings.json excluded via .gitignore with a safe template committed
Auth flow documented in README or inline comments
acceptance_criteria: Secret scanner runs in CI and blocks merge on detected secrets
local.settings.json is in .gitignore; local.settings.json.template is committed
No API keys, passwords, or connection strings appear in any committed file
All Azure SDK clients use DefaultAzureCredential exclusively
Auth approach documented
risks: Developers may inadvertently commit local.settings.json with real credentials during development

tasks.title: Configure Application Insights logging and alerting
tasks.description: Wire the Function App to Application Insights using the APPLICATIONINSIGHTS_CONNECTION_STRING app setting (no instrumentation key). Implement structured logging in the function code (player count, blob URL, run duration). Create an alert rule for function execution failures.
inputs: Azure Monitor alert rule API or portal
outputs: Structured log output visible in Application Insights Live Metrics and Log Analytics
Alert rule firing on function failure (e.g., exceptions or non-zero result code)
Alert routed to email or Teams channel
acceptance_criteria: Function logs contain: invocation ID, player count returned, blob name written, duration in ms
Logs queryable in Log Analytics workspace via KQL
Alert rule fires within 5 minutes of a simulated function failure
Connection string (not instrumentation key) used for Application Insights
risks: Alert notification channel (email/Teams) not defined in spec — requires owner input

tasks.title: Implement retry logic and failure handling
tasks.description: Add retry logic with exponential back-off for TRAPI calls and blob write operations. Ensure the function is idempotent (re-running produces the same blob output). Handle partial failures (e.g., TRAPI succeeds but blob write fails) with appropriate logging and re-raise.
outputs: Updated modules with retry decorators or azure-core RetryPolicy
Idempotent blob overwrite behavior confirmed
Integration tests for retry scenarios
acceptance_criteria: TRAPI client retries up to 3 times with exponential back-off on transient errors (429, 503)
Blob writer retries up to 3 times on transient storage errors
Function is idempotent: running twice produces the same blob with the same content
Partial failure (e.g., TRAPI succeeds, blob fails) logs error and raises exception to trigger Azure retry/alert
Integration tests simulate transient failures and verify retry behavior
risks: Unbounded retries on a timer trigger could cause overlapping executions — cap total retry window well under 24h

tasks.title: Write unit and integration tests
tasks.description: Write comprehensive pytest unit tests for all modules (TRAPI client, blob writer, function handler) with mocked external dependencies. Write integration tests that run against real Azure resources in a test environment to validate end-to-end data flow.
inputs: Test Azure environment (same subscription, separate resource group)
outputs: tests/ directory with unit and integration test suites
pytest passing in CI (unit tests) and on-demand (integration tests)
Test coverage report
acceptance_criteria: Unit test coverage >= 80% for all application modules
All unit tests pass in CI without real Azure credentials
Integration tests invoke the function against real Azure resources and confirm blob is written
Integration tests validate blob content contains expected 1985 Yankees player names
Test results published as CI artifact
risks: Integration tests require a live Azure environment — may be skipped in PR CI and run only on merge

tasks.title: End-to-end production validation and rollout
tasks.description: Deploy to production, manually invoke the function to validate the full flow, confirm the blob is written with the correct roster, verify monitoring is active, and sign off against all spec acceptance criteria.
inputs: Production Function App deployed via CI-01
outputs: Signed-off acceptance criteria checklist
Blob with 1985 Yankees roster present in production Storage Account
Monitoring dashboard / alert confirmed active
acceptance_criteria: Manual function invoke produces a valid JSON blob in production Storage Account
Blob contains all key members of the 1985 New York Yankees roster
Nightly timer trigger confirmed scheduled (next run visible in Azure portal)
Application Insights shows successful run telemetry
All spec acceptance criteria checked off
No API keys present in any deployed configuration
risks: GPT-4o roster completeness cannot be deterministically guaranteed — establish a minimum expected player count for acceptance
